|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=神戸新聞NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション|766190/1/21|山陽鉄道旅客案内 : 一名・山陽道名所図絵|format=EXTERNAL}}。古くは、あまじんじゃ・たるみじんじゃ、日向大明神、衣財田大明神。「ワタツミ」の読みは{{jalink|本居宣長|Q384647}}の説に基づき{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}4年（{{jalink|1871年|Q7746}}）に採用したものである。『播磨国官幣中社海神社史』では「古例の通りアマもしくはタルミと読むべきである」としている。「タルミ」の読みは、祭神の本来の名称が垂水神であったことによるものである。「アマ」は、当社が海直（あまのあたい）の{{jalink|氏神|Q767655}}であったことによる。 == 祭神 == {{jalink|ワタツミ|Q1474187|海神}}三座として以下の3柱を主祭神とし、{{jalink|天照大神|Q455602|大日孁貴尊}}を配祀する。 *上津綿津見神（うわつわたつみのかみ）：海上＝航海の神 *中津綿津見神（なかつわたつみのかみ）：海中＝魚（漁業）の神 *底津綿津見神（そこつわたつみのかみ）：海底＝海藻、塩の神 == 歴史 == 社伝によると、{{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}}が{{jalink|三韓征伐|Q11249435}}からの帰途、当地の海上で暴風雨が起こって船が進めなくなったため、皇后が綿津見三神を祀ると暴風雨が治まり、その縁でこの地に綿津見三神を祀る社殿を建てたのが始まりという。 『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』に記される{{jalink|廣田神社|Q1466105|広田神社}}・{{jalink|生田神社|Q710086}}・{{jalink|長田神社|Q661395}}・{{jalink|住吉大社|Q705949}}創建の記述とほぼ同様であるが、『日本書紀』の当該箇所に海神社に関する記述はない。 文献に現れる最も古い記述は、{{jalink|大同 (日本)|Q1373191|大同}}元年（{{jalink|806年|Q30021}}）の『{{jalink|新抄格勅符抄|Q11502312}}』にある播磨明石垂水神に{{jalink|神封戸}}10戸を寄進するという記述。 当所は海上交通の要地であることから、古くから海上鎮護の神として崇敬を受けた。 『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』では「播磨国{{jalink|明石郡|Q11512561}} 海神社三座」と記載され、名神大社に列している。 中世以降、戦乱等のために社勢が衰えるが、{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}11年（{{jalink|1587年|Q6744}}）に{{jalink|豊臣秀吉|Q187550}}が祈祷料として垂水郷山内の山林を寄進、江戸時代にも歴代{{jalink|明石藩|Q839406}}主が篤く崇敬し、毎年2月に参拝するのを通例としていた。 江戸時代の初頭より「日向大明神」{{Cite book|和書|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=播州名所巡覧図会 二|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=垂水神社 日向明神}}と呼ばれていたが、明治4年（1871年）に{{jalink|国幣中社}}に列格した際に「海神社」に復称した。明治30年（{{jalink|1897年|Q7847}}）に官幣中社に昇格した。 高度成長期前までは大鳥居周辺は砂浜であり、文字通り「海の神社」であったが、1960年以降埋め立てが行われて、大鳥居の前面は道路や漁港となった{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=垂水には砂浜、舞子は松が少しだけあった　昔と今、なぜ変わったの？ |publisher=神戸新聞NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}。 == 祭祀 == === えびす祭 === {{jalink|西宮神社|Q705297}}と同じく{{jalink|えびす|Q1129330}}は海の神でもあるため毎年{{jalink|1月10日|Q2243}}の前後の3日間行われる。 == 前後の札所 == ; {{jalink|神仏霊場巡拝の道|Q10444949}} : 72 {{jalink|須磨寺|Q11664679}} - '''73 海神社''' - 74 {{jalink|広峯神社|Q11484399|廣峯神社}} == その他 == {{jalink|平成|Q234510}}18年（{{jalink|2006年|Q2021}}）まで、毎年{{jalink|7月|Q121}}に行われる夏祭りで奉納{{jalink|花火|Q127933}}大会が開催されていたが、翌年以降夏祭りはこれまで通り開催されるものの奉納花火大会は開催されなくなった。{{要出典範囲|date=2012年1月|平成13年（2001年）に起きた{{jalink|明石花火大会歩道橋事故|Q4700349}}が少なからず影響している。}} == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連図書 == * 安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』{{jalink|神社新報|Q11590705}}社、1968年、65頁 * {{jalink|白井永二|Q11579387}}・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』{{jalink|東京堂出版|Q11524645}}、1979年、359頁 == 関連項目 == *{{jalink|垂水区|Q1078806}} *{{jalink|垂水駅|Q774385}} *{{jalink|マリンピア神戸|Q11341124}}・{{jalink|三井アウトレットパーク マリンピア神戸|Q11354184}} *{{jalink|プリコ垂水|Q28683734}} *{{jalink|下畑海神社}}（元宮） == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Watatsumi-jinja (Kobe)}} * [http://kaijinjya.main.jp/index.php?FrontPage 海神社]（神社公式） * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301189.html 海神社]（兵庫県神社庁） * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/440501.html 海神社三座]（國學院大學21世紀COEプログラム「神道・神社史料集成」） ファイル:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja torii (1).jpg|大鳥居 ファイル:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja altar.jpg|海神社　寳ノ海神 {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{shinto-stub}} [[category:神戸市の神社]] ==End Japanese== [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:播磨国の式内社|名わたつみしんしや]] [[Category:官幣中社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:垂水区の歴史]] [[Category:垂水区の建築物]] [[Category:西垂水]] [[Category:神仏霊場巡拝の道]]

category|Category:Watatsumi-jinja (Kobe)}} [[EN:Watatsumi Shrine (Kobe)]] [[JA:海神社 (神戸市)]] [[SIMPLE:Watatsumi Shrine (Kobe)]] {{wikidata link|Q11559098}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 海神社 |画像 = [[File:Watatsumi-jinja (Tarumi, Kobe), haiden.jpg|270px]]
拝殿 |所在地 = {{jalink|兵庫県|Q130290}}{{jalink|神戸市|Q48320}}{{jalink|垂水区|Q1078806}}宮本町5-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 上津綿津見神
中津綿津見神
底津綿津見神 |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（{{jalink|名神大社|Q9610964|名神大}}）
旧{{jalink|官幣中社}}
{{jalink|別表神社|Q10898274}} |創建 = （伝）{{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}}朝 |本殿 = 三間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}}銅板葺 |別名 = 日向大明神 |札所等 = {{jalink|神仏霊場巡拝の道|Q10444949}}第73番（兵庫第8番）
播磨三大社 |例祭 = {{jalink|10月11日|Q2927}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=海神社|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''海神社'''（わたつみじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|兵庫県|Q130290}}{{jalink|神戸市|Q48320}}{{jalink|垂水区|Q1078806}}宮本町にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}} ({{jalink|名神大社|Q9610964}})で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|官幣中社}}。現在は{{jalink|神社本庁|Q908077}}の{{jalink|別表神社|Q10898274}}。{{jalink|伊和神社|Q65320}}・{{jalink|粒坐天照神社|Q11604472}}とともに{{jalink|播磨|Q3783364}}三大社とされる。 === 概要 === 宗教法人法による届け出では「わたつみじんじゃ」とされているが、「かいじんじゃ」ともいう読みも地元住民や氏子を含め広く知られている{{Cite web |title=神戸の「海神社」なんて読む？ 「かい」「あま」「わたつみ」… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=神戸新聞NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション|766190/1/21|山陽鉄道旅客案内 : 一名・山陽道名所図絵|format=EXTERNAL}}。古くは、あまじんじゃ・たるみじんじゃ、日向大明神、衣財田大明神。「ワタツミ」の読みは{{jalink|本居宣長|Q384647}}の説に基づき{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}4年（{{jalink|1871年|Q7746}}）に採用したものである。『播磨国官幣中社海神社史』では「古例の通りアマもしくはタルミと読むべきである」としている。「タルミ」の読みは、祭神の本来の名称が垂水神であったことによるものである。「アマ」は、当社が海直（あまのあたい）の{{jalink|氏神|Q767655}}であったことによる。 === 祭神 == = {{jalink|ワタツミ|Q1474187|海神}}三座として以下の3柱を主祭神とし、{{jalink|天照大神|Q455602|大日孁貴尊}}を配祀する。 *上津綿津見神（うわつわたつみのかみ）：海上＝航海の神 *中津綿津見神（なかつわたつみのかみ）：海中＝魚（漁業）の神 *底津綿津見神（そこつわたつみのかみ）：海底＝海藻、塩の神 === 歴史 === 社伝によると、{{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}}が{{jalink|三韓征伐|Q11249435}}からの帰途、当地の海上で暴風雨が起こって船が進めなくなったため、皇后が綿津見三神を祀ると暴風雨が治まり、その縁でこの地に綿津見三神を祀る社殿を建てたのが始まりという。 『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』に記される{{jalink|廣田神社|Q1466105|広田神社}}・{{jalink|生田神社|Q710086}}・{{jalink|長田神社|Q661395}}・{{jalink|住吉大社|Q705949}}創建の記述とほぼ同様であるが、『日本書紀』の当該箇所に海神社に関する記述はない。 文献に現れる最も古い記述は、{{jalink|大同 (日本)|Q1373191|大同}}元年（{{jalink|806年|Q30021}}）の『{{jalink|新抄格勅符抄|Q11502312}}』にある播磨明石垂水神に{{jalink|神封戸}}10戸を寄進するという記述。 当所は海上交通の要地であることから、古くから海上鎮護の神として崇敬を受けた。 『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』では「播磨国{{jalink|明石郡|Q11512561}} 海神社三座」と記載され、名神大社に列している。 中世以降、戦乱等のために社勢が衰えるが、{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}11年（{{jalink|1587年|Q6744}}）に{{jalink|豊臣秀吉|Q187550}}が祈祷料として垂水郷山内の山林を寄進、江戸時代にも歴代{{jalink|明石藩|Q839406}}主が篤く崇敬し、毎年2月に参拝するのを通例としていた。 江戸時代の初頭より「日向大明神」{{Cite book|和書|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=播州名所巡覧図会 二|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=垂水神社 日向明神}}と呼ばれていたが、明治4年（1871年）に{{jalink|国幣中社}}に列格した際に「海神社」に復称した。明治30年（{{jalink|1897年|Q7847}}）に官幣中社に昇格した。 高度成長期前までは大鳥居周辺は砂浜であり、文字通り「海の神社」であったが、1960年以降埋め立てが行われて、大鳥居の前面は道路や漁港となった{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=垂水には砂浜、舞子は松が少しだけあった　昔と今、なぜ変わったの？ |publisher=神戸新聞NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}。 === 祭祀 === === えびす祭 === {{jalink|西宮神社|Q705297}}と同じく{{jalink|えびす|Q1129330}}は海の神でもあるため毎年{{jalink|1月10日|Q2243}}の前後の3日間行われる。 === 前後の札所 === ; {{jalink|神仏霊場巡拝の道|Q10444949}} : 72 {{jalink|須磨寺|Q11664679}} - '''73 海神社''' - 74 {{jalink|広峯神社|Q11484399|廣峯神社}} === その他 === {{jalink|平成|Q234510}}18年（{{jalink|2006年|Q2021}}）まで、毎年{{jalink|7月|Q121}}に行われる夏祭りで奉納{{jalink|花火|Q127933}}大会が開催されていたが、翌年以降夏祭りはこれまで通り開催されるものの奉納花火大会は開催されなくなった。{{要出典範囲|date=2012年1月|平成13年（2001年）に起きた{{jalink|明石花火大会歩道橋事故|Q4700349}}が少なからず影響している。}} === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === 関連図書 === * 安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』{{jalink|神社新報|Q11590705}}社、1968年、65頁 * {{jalink|白井永二|Q11579387}}・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』{{jalink|東京堂出版|Q11524645}}、1979年、359頁 === 関連項目 === *{{jalink|垂水区|Q1078806}} *{{jalink|垂水駅|Q774385}} *{{jalink|マリンピア神戸|Q11341124}}・{{jalink|三井アウトレットパーク マリンピア神戸|Q11354184}} *{{jalink|プリコ垂水|Q28683734}} *{{jalink|下畑海神社}}（元宮） === 外部リンク === 海神社三座]（國學院大學21世紀COEプログラム「神道・神社史料集成」） ファイル:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja torii (1).jpg|大鳥居 ファイル:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja altar.jpg|海神社　寳ノ海神 {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{shinto-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:わたつみしんしや}} == Categories category|Category:Watatsumi-jinja (Kobe)}} [[EN:Watatsumi Shrine (Kobe)]] [[JA:海神社 (神戸市)]] [[SIMPLE:Watatsumi Shrine (Kobe)]] {{wikidata link|Q11559098}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 海神社 |画像 = [[File:Watatsumi-jinja (Tarumi, Kobe), haiden.jpg|270px]]
拝殿 |所在地 = {{jalink|兵庫県|Q130290}}{{jalink|神戸市|Q48320}}{{jalink|垂水区|Q1078806}}宮本町5-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 上津綿津見神
中津綿津見神
底津綿津見神 |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（{{jalink|名神大社|Q9610964|名神大}}）
旧{{jalink|官幣中社}}
{{jalink|別表神社|Q10898274}} |創建 = （伝）{{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}}朝 |本殿 = 三間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}}銅板葺 |別名 = 日向大明神 |札所等 = {{jalink|神仏霊場巡拝の道|Q10444949}}第73番（兵庫第8番）
播磨三大社 |例祭 = {{jalink|10月11日|Q2927}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=海神社|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''海神社'''（わたつみじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|兵庫県|Q130290}}{{jalink|神戸市|Q48320}}{{jalink|垂水区|Q1078806}}宮本町にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}} ({{jalink|名神大社|Q9610964}})で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|官幣中社}}。現在は{{jalink|神社本庁|Q908077}}の{{jalink|別表神社|Q10898274}}。{{jalink|伊和神社|Q65320}}・{{jalink|粒坐天照神社|Q11604472}}とともに{{jalink|播磨|Q3783364}}三大社とされる。 === 概要 === 宗教法人法による届け出では「わたつみじんじゃ」とされているが、「かいじんじゃ」ともいう読みも地元住民や氏子を含め広く知られている{{Cite web |title=神戸の「海神社」なんて読む？ 「かい」「あま」「わたつみ」… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=神戸新聞NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション|766190/1/21|山陽鉄道旅客案内 : 一名・山陽道名所図絵|format=EXTERNAL}}。古くは、あまじんじゃ・たるみじんじゃ、日向大明神、衣財田大明神。「ワタツミ」の読みは{{jalink|本居宣長|Q384647}}の説に基づき{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}4年（{{jalink|1871年|Q7746}}）に採用したものである。『播磨国官幣中社海神社史』では「古例の通りアマもしくはタルミと読むべきである」としている。「タルミ」の読みは、祭神の本来の名称が垂水神であったことによるものである。「アマ」は、当社が海直（あまのあたい）の{{jalink|氏神|Q767655}}であったことによる。 === 祭神 === {{jalink|ワタツミ|Q1474187|海神}}三座として以下の3柱を主祭神とし、{{jalink|天照大神|Q455602|大日孁貴尊}}を配祀する。 *上津綿津見神（うわつわたつみのかみ）：海上＝航海の神 *中津綿津見神（なかつわたつみのかみ）：海中＝魚（漁業）の神 *底津綿津見神（そこつわたつみのかみ）：海底＝海藻、塩の神 === 歴史 === 社伝によると、{{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}}が{{jalink|三韓征伐|Q11249435}}からの帰途、当地の海上で暴風雨が起こって船が進めなくなったため、皇后が綿津見三神を祀ると暴風雨が治まり、その縁でこの地に綿津見三神を祀る社殿を建てたのが始まりという。 『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』に記される{{jalink|廣田神社|Q1466105|広田神社}}・{{jalink|生田神社|Q710086}}・{{jalink|長田神社|Q661395}}・{{jalink|住吉大社|Q705949}}創建の記述とほぼ同様であるが、『日本書紀』の当該箇所に海神社に関する記述はない。 文献に現れる最も古い記述は、{{jalink|大同 (日本)|Q1373191|大同}}元年（{{jalink|806年|Q30021}}）の『{{jalink|新抄格勅符抄|Q11502312}}』にある播磨明石垂水神に{{jalink|神封戸}}10戸を寄進するという記述。 当所は海上交通の要地であることから、古くから海上鎮護の神として崇敬を受けた。 『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』では「播磨国{{jalink|明石郡|Q11512561}} 海神社三座」と記載され、名神大社に列している。 中世以降、戦乱等のために社勢が衰えるが、{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}11年（{{jalink|1587年|Q6744}}）に{{jalink|豊臣秀吉|Q187550}}が祈祷料として垂水郷山内の山林を寄進、江戸時代にも歴代{{jalink|明石藩|Q839406}}主が篤く崇敬し、毎年2月に参拝するのを通例としていた。 江戸時代の初頭より「日向大明神」{{Cite book|和書|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=播州名所巡覧図会 二|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=垂水神社 日向明神}}と呼ばれていたが、明治4年（1871年）に{{jalink|国幣中社}}に列格した際に「海神社」に復称した。明治30年（{{jalink|1897年|Q7847}}）に官幣中社に昇格した。 高度成長期前までは大鳥居周辺は砂浜であり、文字通り「海の神社」であったが、1960年以降埋め立てが行われて、大鳥居の前面は道路や漁港となった{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=垂水には砂浜、舞子は松が少しだけあった　昔と今、なぜ変わったの？ |publisher=神戸新聞NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}。 === 祭祀 === === えびす祭 === {{jalink|西宮神社|Q705297}}と同じく{{jalink|えびす|Q1129330}}は海の神でもあるため毎年{{jalink|1月10日|Q2243}}の前後の3日間行われる。 === 前後の札所 === ; {{jalink|神仏霊場巡拝の道|Q10444949}} : 72 {{jalink|須磨寺|Q11664679}} - '''73 海神社''' - 74 {{jalink|広峯神社|Q11484399|廣峯神社}} === その他 === {{jalink|平成|Q234510}}18年（{{jalink|2006年|Q2021}}）まで、毎年{{jalink|7月|Q121}}に行われる夏祭りで奉納{{jalink|花火|Q127933}}大会が開催されていたが、翌年以降夏祭りはこれまで通り開催されるものの奉納花火大会は開催されなくなった。{{要出典範囲|date=2012年1月|平成13年（2001年）に起きた{{jalink|明石花火大会歩道橋事故|Q4700349}}が少なからず影響している。}} === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === 関連図書 === * 安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』{{jalink|神社新報|Q11590705}}社、1968年、65頁 * {{jalink|白井永二|Q11579387}}・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』{{jalink|東京堂出版|Q11524645}}、1979年、359頁 === 関連項目 === *{{jalink|垂水区|Q1078806}} *{{jalink|垂水駅|Q774385}} *{{jalink|マリンピア神戸|Q11341124}}・{{jalink|三井アウトレットパーク マリンピア神戸|Q11354184}} *{{jalink|プリコ垂水|Q28683734}} *{{jalink|下畑海神社}}（元宮） === 外部リンク === 海神社三座]（國學院大學21世紀COEプログラム「神道・神社史料集成」） ファイル:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja torii (1).jpg|大鳥居 ファイル:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja altar.jpg|海神社　寳ノ海神 {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{shinto-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:わたつみしんしや}} == Categories category|Category:Watatsumi-jinja (Kobe)}} [[EN:Watatsumi Shrine (Kobe)]] [[JA:海神社 (神戸市)]] [[SIMPLE:Watatsumi Shrine (Kobe)]] {{wikidata link|Q11559098}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 海神社 |画像 = [[File:Watatsumi-jinja (Tarumi, Kobe), haiden.jpg|270px]]
拝殿 |所在地 = {{jalink|兵庫県|Q130290}}{{jalink|神戸市|Q48320}}{{jalink|垂水区|Q1078806}}宮本町5-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 上津綿津見神
中津綿津見神
底津綿津見神 |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（{{jalink|名神大社|Q9610964|名神大}}）
旧{{jalink|官幣中社}}
{{jalink|別表神社|Q10898274}} |創建 = （伝）{{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}}朝 |本殿 = 三間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}}銅板葺 |別名 = 日向大明神 |札所等 = {{jalink|神仏霊場巡拝の道|Q10444949}}第73番（兵庫第8番）
播磨三大社 |例祭 = {{jalink|10月11日|Q2927}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=海神社|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''海神社'''（わたつみじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|兵庫県|Q130290}}{{jalink|神戸市|Q48320}}{{jalink|垂水区|Q1078806}}宮本町にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}} ({{jalink|名神大社|Q9610964}})で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|官幣中社}}。現在は{{jalink|神社本庁|Q908077}}の{{jalink|別表神社|Q10898274}}。{{jalink|伊和神社|Q65320}}・{{jalink|粒坐天照神社|Q11604472}}とともに{{jalink|播磨|Q3783364}}三大社とされる。 === 概要 === 宗教法人法による届け出では「わたつみじんじゃ」とされているが、「かいじんじゃ」ともいう読みも地元住民や氏子を含め広く知られている{{Cite web |title=神戸の「海神社」なんて読む？ 「かい」「あま」「わたつみ」… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=神戸新聞NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション|766190/1/21|山陽鉄道旅客案内 : 一名・山陽道名所図絵|format=EXTERNAL}}。古くは、あまじんじゃ・たるみじんじゃ、日向大明神、衣財田大明神。「ワタツミ」の読みは{{jalink|本居宣長|Q384647}}の説に基づき{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}4年（{{jalink|1871年|Q7746}}）に採用したものである。『播磨国官幣中社海神社史』では「古例の通りアマもしくはタルミと読むべきである」としている。「タルミ」の読みは、祭神の本来の名称が垂水神であったことによるものである。「アマ」は、当社が海直（あまのあたい）の{{jalink|氏神|Q767655}}であったことによる。 === 祭神 === {{jalink|ワタツミ|Q1474187|海神}}三座として以下の3柱を主祭神とし、{{jalink|天照大神|Q455602|大日孁貴尊}}を配祀する。 *上津綿津見神（うわつわたつみのかみ）：海上＝航海の神 *中津綿津見神（なかつわたつみのかみ）：海中＝魚（漁業）の神 *底津綿津見神（そこつわたつみのかみ）：海底＝海藻、塩の神 === 歴史 === 社伝によると、{{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}}が{{jalink|三韓征伐|Q11249435}}からの帰途、当地の海上で暴風雨が起こって船が進めなくなったため、皇后が綿津見三神を祀ると暴風雨が治まり、その縁でこの地に綿津見三神を祀る社殿を建てたのが始まりという。 『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』に記される{{jalink|廣田神社|Q1466105|広田神社}}・{{jalink|生田神社|Q710086}}・{{jalink|長田神社|Q661395}}・{{jalink|住吉大社|Q705949}}創建の記述とほぼ同様であるが、『日本書紀』の当該箇所に海神社に関する記述はない。 文献に現れる最も古い記述は、{{jalink|大同 (日本)|Q1373191|大同}}元年（{{jalink|806年|Q30021}}）の『{{jalink|新抄格勅符抄|Q11502312}}』にある播磨明石垂水神に{{jalink|神封戸}}10戸を寄進するという記述。 当所は海上交通の要地であることから、古くから海上鎮護の神として崇敬を受けた。 『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』では「播磨国{{jalink|明石郡|Q11512561}} 海神社三座」と記載され、名神大社に列している。 中世以降、戦乱等のために社勢が衰えるが、{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}11年（{{jalink|1587年|Q6744}}）に{{jalink|豊臣秀吉|Q187550}}が祈祷料として垂水郷山内の山林を寄進、江戸時代にも歴代{{jalink|明石藩|Q839406}}主が篤く崇敬し、毎年2月に参拝するのを通例としていた。 江戸時代の初頭より「日向大明神」{{Cite book|和書|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=播州名所巡覧図会 二|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=垂水神社 日向明神}}と呼ばれていたが、明治4年（1871年）に{{jalink|国幣中社}}に列格した際に「海神社」に復称した。明治30年（{{jalink|1897年|Q7847}}）に官幣中社に昇格した。 高度成長期前までは大鳥居周辺は砂浜であり、文字通り「海の神社」であったが、1960年以降埋め立てが行われて、大鳥居の前面は道路や漁港となった{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=垂水には砂浜、舞子は松が少しだけあった　昔と今、なぜ変わったの？ |publisher=神戸新聞NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}。 === 祭祀 === === えびす祭 === {{jalink|西宮神社|Q705297}}と同じく{{jalink|えびす|Q1129330}}は海の神でもあるため毎年{{jalink|1月10日|Q2243}}の前後の3日間行われる。 === 前後の札所 === ; {{jalink|神仏霊場巡拝の道|Q10444949}} : 72 {{jalink|須磨寺|Q11664679}} - '''73 海神社''' - 74 {{jalink|広峯神社|Q11484399|廣峯神社}} === その他 === {{jalink|平成|Q234510}}18年（{{jalink|2006年|Q2021}}）まで、毎年{{jalink|7月|Q121}}に行われる夏祭りで奉納{{jalink|花火|Q127933}}大会が開催されていたが、翌年以降夏祭りはこれまで通り開催されるものの奉納花火大会は開催されなくなった。{{要出典範囲|date=2012年1月|平成13年（2001年）に起きた{{jalink|明石花火大会歩道橋事故|Q4700349}}が少なからず影響している。}} === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === 関連図書 === * 安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』{{jalink|神社新報|Q11590705}}社、1968年、65頁 * {{jalink|白井永二|Q11579387}}・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』{{jalink|東京堂出版|Q11524645}}、1979年、359頁 === 関連項目 === *{{jalink|垂水区|Q1078806}} *{{jalink|垂水駅|Q774385}} *{{jalink|マリンピア神戸|Q11341124}}・{{jalink|三井アウトレットパーク マリンピア神戸|Q11354184}} *{{jalink|プリコ垂水|Q28683734}} *{{jalink|下畑海神社}}（元宮） === 外部リンク === 海神社三座]（國學院大學21世紀COEプログラム「神道・神社史料集成」） ファイル:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja torii (1).jpg|大鳥居 ファイル:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja altar.jpg|海神社　寳ノ海神 {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{shinto-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:わたつみしんしや}} == Categories 中津綿津見神
底津綿津見神 |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（{{jalink|名神大社|Q9610964|名神大}}）
旧{{jalink|官幣中社}}
{{jalink|別表神社|Q10898274}} |創建 = （伝）{{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}}朝 |本殿 = 三間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}}銅板葺 |別名 = 日向大明神 |札所等 = {{jalink|神仏霊場巡拝の道|Q10444949}}第73番（兵庫第8番）
播磨三大社 |例祭 = {{jalink|10月11日|Q2927}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=海神社|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''海神社'''（わたつみじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|兵庫県|Q130290}}{{jalink|神戸市|Q48320}}{{jalink|垂水区|Q1078806}}宮本町にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}} ({{jalink|名神大社|Q9610964}})で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|官幣中社}}。現在は{{jalink|神社本庁|Q908077}}の{{jalink|別表神社|Q10898274}}。{{jalink|伊和神社|Q65320}}・{{jalink|粒坐天照神社|Q11604472}}とともに{{jalink|播磨|Q3783364}}三大社とされる。 === 概要 === 宗教法人法による届け出では「わたつみじんじゃ」とされているが、「かいじんじゃ」ともいう読みも地元住民や氏子を含め広く知られている{{Cite web |title=神戸の「海神社」なんて読む？ 「かい」「あま」「わたつみ」… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=神戸新聞NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション|766190/1/21|山陽鉄道旅客案内 : 一名・山陽道名所図絵|format=EXTERNAL}}。古くは、あまじんじゃ・たるみじんじゃ、日向大明神、衣財田大明神。「ワタツミ」の読みは{{jalink|本居宣長|Q384647}}の説に基づき{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}4年（{{jalink|1871年|Q7746}}）に採用したものである。『播磨国官幣中社海神社史』では「古例の通りアマもしくはタルミと読むべきである」としている。「タルミ」の読みは、祭神の本来の名称が垂水神であったことによるものである。「アマ」は、当社が海直（あまのあたい）の{{jalink|氏神|Q767655}}であったことによる。 === 祭神 === {{jalink|ワタツミ|Q1474187|海神}}三座として以下の3柱を主祭神とし、{{jalink|天照大神|Q455602|大日孁貴尊}}を配祀する。 *上津綿津見神（うわつわたつみのかみ）：海上＝航海の神 *中津綿津見神（なかつわたつみのかみ）：海中＝魚（漁業）の神 *底津綿津見神（そこつわたつみのかみ）：海底＝海藻、塩の神 === 歴史 === 社伝によると、{{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}}が{{jalink|三韓征伐|Q11249435}}からの帰途、当地の海上で暴風雨が起こって船が進めなくなったため、皇后が綿津見三神を祀ると暴風雨が治まり、その縁でこの地に綿津見三神を祀る社殿を建てたのが始まりという。 『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』に記される{{jalink|廣田神社|Q1466105|広田神社}}・{{jalink|生田神社|Q710086}}・{{jalink|長田神社|Q661395}}・{{jalink|住吉大社|Q705949}}創建の記述とほぼ同様であるが、『日本書紀』の当該箇所に海神社に関する記述はない。 文献に現れる最も古い記述は、{{jalink|大同 (日本)|Q1373191|大同}}元年（{{jalink|806年|Q30021}}）の『{{jalink|新抄格勅符抄|Q11502312}}』にある播磨明石垂水神に{{jalink|神封戸}}10戸を寄進するという記述。 当所は海上交通の要地であることから、古くから海上鎮護の神として崇敬を受けた。 『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』では「播磨国{{jalink|明石郡|Q11512561}} 海神社三座」と記載され、名神大社に列している。 中世以降、戦乱等のために社勢が衰えるが、{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}11年（{{jalink|1587年|Q6744}}）に{{jalink|豊臣秀吉|Q187550}}が祈祷料として垂水郷山内の山林を寄進、江戸時代にも歴代{{jalink|明石藩|Q839406}}主が篤く崇敬し、毎年2月に参拝するのを通例としていた。 江戸時代の初頭より「日向大明神」{{Cite book|和書|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=播州名所巡覧図会 二|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=垂水神社 日向明神}}と呼ばれていたが、明治4年（1871年）に{{jalink|国幣中社}}に列格した際に「海神社」に復称した。明治30年（{{jalink|1897年|Q7847}}）に官幣中社に昇格した。 高度成長期前までは大鳥居周辺は砂浜であり、文字通り「海の神社」であったが、1960年以降埋め立てが行われて、大鳥居の前面は道路や漁港となった{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=垂水には砂浜、舞子は松が少しだけあった　昔と今、なぜ変わったの？ |publisher=神戸新聞NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}。 === 祭祀 === === えびす祭 === {{jalink|西宮神社|Q705297}}と同じく{{jalink|えびす|Q1129330}}は海の神でもあるため毎年{{jalink|1月10日|Q2243}}の前後の3日間行われる。 === 前後の札所 === ; {{jalink|神仏霊場巡拝の道|Q10444949}} : 72 {{jalink|須磨寺|Q11664679}} - '''73 海神社''' - 74 {{jalink|広峯神社|Q11484399|廣峯神社}} === その他 === {{jalink|平成|Q234510}}18年（{{jalink|2006年|Q2021}}）まで、毎年{{jalink|7月|Q121}}に行われる夏祭りで奉納{{jalink|花火|Q127933}}大会が開催されていたが、翌年以降夏祭りはこれまで通り開催されるものの奉納花火大会は開催されなくなった。{{要出典範囲|date=2012年1月|平成13年（2001年）に起きた{{jalink|明石花火大会歩道橋事故|Q4700349}}が少なからず影響している。}} === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === 関連図書 === * 安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』{{jalink|神社新報|Q11590705}}社、1968年、65頁 * {{jalink|白井永二|Q11579387}}・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』{{jalink|東京堂出版|Q11524645}}、1979年、359頁 === 関連項目 === *{{jalink|垂水区|Q1078806}} *{{jalink|垂水駅|Q774385}} *{{jalink|マリンピア神戸|Q11341124}}・{{jalink|三井アウトレットパーク マリンピア神戸|Q11354184}} *{{jalink|プリコ垂水|Q28683734}} *{{jalink|下畑海神社}}（元宮） === 外部リンク === 海神社三座]（國學院大學21世紀COEプログラム「神道・神社史料集成」） ファイル:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja torii (1).jpg|大鳥居 ファイル:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja altar.jpg|海神社　寳ノ海神 {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{shinto-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:わたつみしんしや}} == Categories 一名・山陽道名所図絵|format=EXTERNAL}}。古くは、あまじんじゃ・たるみじんじゃ、日向大明神、衣財田大明神。「ワタツミ」の読みは{{jalink|本居宣長|Q384647}}の説に基づき{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}4年（{{jalink|1871年|Q7746}}）に採用したものである。『播磨国官幣中社海神社史』では「古例の通りアマもしくはタルミと読むべきである」としている。「タルミ」の読みは、祭神の本来の名称が垂水神であったことによるものである。「アマ」は、当社が海直（あまのあたい）の{{jalink|氏神|Q767655}}であったことによる。 === 祭神 === {{jalink|ワタツミ|Q1474187|海神}}三座として以下の3柱を主祭神とし、{{jalink|天照大神|Q455602|大日孁貴尊}}を配祀する。 *上津綿津見神（うわつわたつみのかみ）：海上＝航海の神 *中津綿津見神（なかつわたつみのかみ）：海中＝魚（漁業）の神 *底津綿津見神（そこつわたつみのかみ）：海底＝海藻、塩の神 === 歴史 === 社伝によると、{{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}}が{{jalink|三韓征伐|Q11249435}}からの帰途、当地の海上で暴風雨が起こって船が進めなくなったため、皇后が綿津見三神を祀ると暴風雨が治まり、その縁でこの地に綿津見三神を祀る社殿を建てたのが始まりという。 『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』に記される{{jalink|廣田神社|Q1466105|広田神社}}・{{jalink|生田神社|Q710086}}・{{jalink|長田神社|Q661395}}・{{jalink|住吉大社|Q705949}}創建の記述とほぼ同様であるが、『日本書紀』の当該箇所に海神社に関する記述はない。 文献に現れる最も古い記述は、{{jalink|大同 (日本)|Q1373191|大同}}元年（{{jalink|806年|Q30021}}）の『{{jalink|新抄格勅符抄|Q11502312}}』にある播磨明石垂水神に{{jalink|神封戸}}10戸を寄進するという記述。 当所は海上交通の要地であることから、古くから海上鎮護の神として崇敬を受けた。 『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』では「播磨国{{jalink|明石郡|Q11512561}} 海神社三座」と記載され、名神大社に列している。 中世以降、戦乱等のために社勢が衰えるが、{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}11年（{{jalink|1587年|Q6744}}）に{{jalink|豊臣秀吉|Q187550}}が祈祷料として垂水郷山内の山林を寄進、江戸時代にも歴代{{jalink|明石藩|Q839406}}主が篤く崇敬し、毎年2月に参拝するのを通例としていた。 江戸時代の初頭より「日向大明神」{{Cite book|和書|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=播州名所巡覧図会 二|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=垂水神社 日向明神}}と呼ばれていたが、明治4年（1871年）に{{jalink|国幣中社}}に列格した際に「海神社」に復称した。明治30年（{{jalink|1897年|Q7847}}）に官幣中社に昇格した。 高度成長期前までは大鳥居周辺は砂浜であり、文字通り「海の神社」であったが、1960年以降埋め立てが行われて、大鳥居の前面は道路や漁港となった{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=垂水には砂浜、舞子は松が少しだけあった　昔と今、なぜ変わったの？ |publisher=神戸新聞NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}。 === 祭祀 === === えびす祭 === {{jalink|西宮神社|Q705297}}と同じく{{jalink|えびす|Q1129330}}は海の神でもあるため毎年{{jalink|1月10日|Q2243}}の前後の3日間行われる。 === 前後の札所 === ; {{jalink|神仏霊場巡拝の道|Q10444949}} : 72 {{jalink|須磨寺|Q11664679}} - '''73 海神社''' - 74 {{jalink|広峯神社|Q11484399|廣峯神社}} === その他 === {{jalink|平成|Q234510}}18年（{{jalink|2006年|Q2021}}）まで、毎年{{jalink|7月|Q121}}に行われる夏祭りで奉納{{jalink|花火|Q127933}}大会が開催されていたが、翌年以降夏祭りはこれまで通り開催されるものの奉納花火大会は開催されなくなった。{{要出典範囲|date=2012年1月|平成13年（2001年）に起きた{{jalink|明石花火大会歩道橋事故|Q4700349}}が少なからず影響している。}} category|Category:Watatsumi-jinja (Kobe)}} [[EN:Watatsumi Shrine (Kobe)]] [[JA:海神社 (神戸市)]] [[SIMPLE:Watatsumi Shrine (Kobe)]] {{wikidata link|Q11559098}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Watatsumi Shrine |Image = [[File:Watatsumi-jinja (Tarumi, Kobe), haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{jalink|Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Kobe City|Q48320}}{{jalink|Tarumi Ward|Q1078806}} Miyamoto-chō 5-1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami
Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami
Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{jalink|Kanpei Chūsha}}
{{jalink|Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274}} |Founded = (Traditionally) during the reign of {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} |Main hall = Sangensha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} style, copper roofing |Other names = Hyūga Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage = {{jalink|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949}} No. 73 (Hyōgo No. 8)
One of the Three Great Shrines of Harima |Annual festival = {{jalink|October 11|Q2927}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Watatsumi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Watatsumi Shrine''' (わたつみじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Miyamoto-chō, {{jalink|Tarumi Ward|Q1078806}}, {{jalink|Kobe City|Q48320}}, {{jalink|Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|former shrine rank}} it was a {{jalink|Kanpei Chūsha}}. Today, it is listed as a {{jalink|Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274}} of the {{jalink|Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077}}. Together with {{jalink|Iwa Shrine|Q65320}} and {{jalink|Tsuburaiza Amateru Shrine|Q11604472}}, it is considered one of the Three Great Shrines of {{jalink|Harima|Q3783364}}. === Overview === In notifications under the Religious Corporations Act, the shrine is read as “Watatsumi Jinja,” but the reading “Kaijinja” is also widely known among local residents and parishioners{{Cite web |title=How do you read Kobe’s “Watatsumi Shrine”? “Kai,” “Ama,” “Watatsumi”… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|766190/1/21|Sanyō Railway Passenger Guide: also called Illustrated Guide to Famous Places along the Sanyō Road|format=EXTERNAL}}. In ancient times it was called Amajinja or Tarumijinja, as well as Hyūga Daimyōjin and Izaida Daimyōjin. The reading “Watatsumi” was adopted in Meiji 4 (1871) based on the theory of {{jalink|Motoori Norinaga|Q384647}}. The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{jalink|Watatsumi|Q1474187|sea deities}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} was returning from the {{jalink|Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}} for {{jalink|Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|Hirota Shrine}}, {{jalink|Ikuta Shrine|Q710086}}, {{jalink|Nagata Shrine|Q661395}}, and {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{jalink|Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{jalink|shinpu|divine land households}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{jalink|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{jalink|Akashi Domain|Q839406}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{jalink|Kokuhei Chūsha}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{jalink|Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297}}, {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{jalink|January 10|Q2243}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{jalink|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949}} : 72 {{jalink|Sumadera|Q11664679}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{jalink|Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|Hirohata Shrine}} === Other === Until {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{jalink|July|Q121}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{jalink|Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and supervised by Motohiko Anzu and Yoshihiko Umeda, *Shinto Dictionary*, {{jalink|Jinja Shinpō|Q11590705}}, category|Category:Watatsumi-jinja (Kobe)}} [[EN:Watatsumi Shrine (Kobe)]] [[JA:海神社 (神戸市)]] [[SIMPLE:Watatsumi Shrine (Kobe)]] {{wikidata link|Q11559098}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Watatsumi Shrine |Image = [[File:Watatsumi-jinja (Tarumi, Kobe), haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{jalink|Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Kobe City|Q48320}}{{jalink|Tarumi Ward|Q1078806}} Miyamoto-chō 5-1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami
Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami
Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{jalink|Kanpei Chūsha}}
{{jalink|Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274}} |Founded = (Traditionally) during the reign of {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} |Main hall = Sangensha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} style, copper roofing |Other names = Hyūga Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage = {{jalink|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949}} No. 73 (Hyōgo No. 8)
One of the Three Great Shrines of Harima |Annual festival = {{jalink|October 11|Q2927}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Watatsumi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Watatsumi Shrine''' (わたつみじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Miyamoto-chō, {{jalink|Tarumi Ward|Q1078806}}, {{jalink|Kobe City|Q48320}}, {{jalink|Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|former shrine rank}} it was a {{jalink|Kanpei Chūsha}}. Today, it is listed as a {{jalink|Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274}} of the {{jalink|Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077}}. Together with {{jalink|Iwa Shrine|Q65320}} and {{jalink|Tsuburaiza Amateru Shrine|Q11604472}}, it is considered one of the Three Great Shrines of {{jalink|Harima|Q3783364}}. === Overview === In notifications under the Religious Corporations Act, the shrine is read as “Watatsumi Jinja,” but the reading “Kaijinja” is also widely known among local residents and parishioners{{Cite web |title=How do you read Kobe’s “Watatsumi Shrine”? “Kai,” “Ama,” “Watatsumi”… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|766190/1/21|Sanyō Railway Passenger Guide: also called Illustrated Guide to Famous Places along the Sanyō Road|format=EXTERNAL}}. In ancient times it was called Amajinja or Tarumijinja, as well as Hyūga Daimyōjin and Izaida Daimyōjin. The reading “Watatsumi” was adopted in Meiji 4 (1871) based on the theory of {{jalink|Motoori Norinaga|Q384647}}. The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{jalink|Watatsumi|Q1474187|sea deities}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} was returning from the {{jalink|Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}} for {{jalink|Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|Hirota Shrine}}, {{jalink|Ikuta Shrine|Q710086}}, {{jalink|Nagata Shrine|Q661395}}, and {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{jalink|Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{jalink|shinpu|divine land households}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{jalink|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{jalink|Akashi Domain|Q839406}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{jalink|Kokuhei Chūsha}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{jalink|Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297}}, {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{jalink|January 10|Q2243}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{jalink|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949}} : 72 {{jalink|Sumadera|Q11664679}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{jalink|Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|Hirohata Shrine}} === Other === Until {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{jalink|July|Q121}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{jalink|Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and supervised by Motohiko Anzu and Yoshihiko Umeda, *Shinto Dictionary*, {{jalink|Jinja Shinpō|Q11590705}}, 1968, p. 65 * Edited by {{jalink|Eiji Shirai|Q11579387}} and Masanori Toki, *Shrine Dictionary*, {{jalink|Tōkyōdō Publishing|Q11524645}}, 1979, p. 359 === Related Articles === * {{jalink|Tarumi Ward|Q1078806}} * {{jalink|Tarumi Station|Q774385}} * {{jalink|Marinepia Kobe|Q11341124}} / {{jalink|Mitsui Outlet Park Marinepia Kobe|Q11354184}} * {{jalink|Prico Tarumi|Q28683734}} * (Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection”) File:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja torii (1).jpg|Great torii gate File:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja {{jalink|Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274}} |Founded = (Traditionally) during the reign of {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} |Main hall = Sangensha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} style, copper roofing |Other names = Hyūga Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage = {{jalink|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949}} No. 73 (Hyōgo No. 8)
One of the Three Great Shrines of Harima |Annual festival = {{jalink|October 11|Q2927}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Watatsumi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Watatsumi Shrine''' (わたつみじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Miyamoto-chō, {{jalink|Tarumi Ward|Q1078806}}, {{jalink|Kobe City|Q48320}}, {{jalink|Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|former shrine rank}} it was a {{jalink|Kanpei Chūsha}}. Today, it is listed as a {{jalink|Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274}} of the {{jalink|Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077}}. Together with {{jalink|Iwa Shrine|Q65320}} and {{jalink|Tsuburaiza Amateru Shrine|Q11604472}}, it is considered one of the Three Great Shrines of {{jalink|Harima|Q3783364}}. === Overview === In notifications under the Religious Corporations Act, the shrine is read as “Watatsumi Jinja,” but the reading “Kaijinja” is also widely known among local residents and parishioners{{Cite web |title=How do you read Kobe’s “Watatsumi Shrine”? “Kai,” “Ama,” “Watatsumi”… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|766190/1/21|Sanyō Railway Passenger Guide: also called Illustrated Guide to Famous Places along the Sanyō Road|format=EXTERNAL}}. In ancient times it was called Amajinja or Tarumijinja, as well as Hyūga Daimyōjin and Izaida Daimyōjin. The reading “Watatsumi” was adopted in Meiji 4 (1871) based on the theory of {{jalink|Motoori Norinaga|Q384647}}. The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{jalink|Watatsumi|Q1474187|sea deities}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} was returning from the {{jalink|Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}} for {{jalink|Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|Hirota Shrine}}, {{jalink|Ikuta Shrine|Q710086}}, {{jalink|Nagata Shrine|Q661395}}, and {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{jalink|Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{jalink|shinpu|divine land households}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{jalink|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{jalink|Akashi Domain|Q839406}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{jalink|Kokuhei Chūsha}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{jalink|Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297}}, {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{jalink|January 10|Q2243}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{jalink|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949}} : 72 {{jalink|Sumadera|Q11664679}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{jalink|Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|Hirohata Shrine}} === Other === Until {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{jalink|July|Q121}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{jalink|Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and supervised by Motohiko Anzu and Yoshihiko Umeda, *Shinto Dictionary*, {{jalink|Jinja Shinpō|Q11590705}}, It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|former shrine rank}} it was a {{jalink|Kanpei Chūsha}}. Together with {{jalink|Iwa Shrine|Q65320}} and {{jalink|Tsuburaiza Amateru Shrine|Q11604472}}, it is considered one of the Three Great Shrines of {{jalink|Harima|Q3783364}}. === Overview === In notifications under the Religious Corporations Act, the shrine is read as “Watatsumi Jinja,” but the reading “Kaijinja” is also widely known among local residents and parishioners{{Cite web |title=How do you read Kobe’s “Watatsumi Shrine”? “Kai,” “Ama,” “Watatsumi”… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|766190/1/21|Sanyō Railway Passenger Guide: also called Illustrated Guide to Famous Places along the Sanyō Road|format=EXTERNAL}}. In ancient times it was called Amajinja or Tarumijinja, as well as Hyūga Daimyōjin and Izaida Daimyōjin. The reading “Watatsumi” was adopted in Meiji 4 (1871) based on the theory of {{jalink|Motoori Norinaga|Q384647}}. The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{jalink|Watatsumi|Q1474187|sea deities}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} was returning from the {{jalink|Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}} for {{jalink|Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|Hirota Shrine}}, {{jalink|Ikuta Shrine|Q710086}}, {{jalink|Nagata Shrine|Q661395}}, and {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{jalink|Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{jalink|shinpu|divine land households}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{jalink|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{jalink|Akashi Domain|Q839406}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{jalink|Kokuhei Chūsha}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{jalink|Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297}}, {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{jalink|January 10|Q2243}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{jalink|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949}} : 72 {{jalink|Sumadera|Q11664679}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{jalink|Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|Hirohata Shrine}} === Other === Until {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{jalink|July|Q121}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{jalink|Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and supervised by Motohiko Anzu and Yoshihiko Umeda, *Shinto Dictionary*, {{jalink|Jinja Shinpō|Q11590705}}, Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}{{ill|lt=Kobe City|Q48320|WD=Q48320}}{{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806}} Miyamoto-chō 5-1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami
Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami
Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami |Shrine rank = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{ill||lt=Kanpei Chūsha|UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274}} |Founded = (Traditionally) during the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} style, copper roofing |Other names = Hyūga Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage = {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949}} No. 73 (Hyōgo No. 8)
One of the Three Great Shrines of Harima |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 11|Q2927|WD=Q2927}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Watatsumi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Watatsumi Shrine''' (わたつみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Miyamoto-chō, {{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe City|Q48320|WD=Q48320}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}), and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank}} it was a {{ill||lt=Kanpei Chūsha|UNKNOWN}}. Today, it is listed as a {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274}} of the {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077|WD=Q908077}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Iwa Shrine|Q65320|WD=Q65320}} and {{ill|lt=Tsuburaiza Amateru Shrine|Q11604472|WD=Q11604472}}, it is considered one of the Three Great Shrines of {{ill|lt=Harima|Q3783364|WD=Q3783364}}. === Overview === In notifications under the Religious Corporations Act, the shrine is read as “Watatsumi Jinja,” but the reading “Kaijinja” is also widely known among local residents and parishioners{{Cite web |title=How do you read Kobe’s “Watatsumi Shrine”? “Kai,” “Ama,” “Watatsumi”… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|766190/1/21|Sanyō Railway Passenger Guide: also called Illustrated Guide to Famous Places along the Sanyō Road|format=EXTERNAL}}. In ancient times it was called Amajinja or Tarumijinja, as well as Hyūga Daimyōjin and Izaida Daimyōjin. The reading “Watatsumi” was adopted in Meiji 4 (1871) based on the theory of {{ill|lt=Motoori Norinaga|Q384647|WD=Q384647}}. The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|WD=Q1474187|lt=sea deities}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} was returning from the {{ill|lt=Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}} for {{ill|lt=Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086}}, {{ill|lt=Nagata Shrine|Q661395|WD=Q661395}}, and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{ill||lt=shinpu|UNKNOWN}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{ill|lt=Akashi Domain|Q839406|WD=Q839406}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{ill||lt=Kokuhei Chūsha|UNKNOWN}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{ill|lt=Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297|WD=Q705297}}, {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{ill|lt=January 10|Q2243|WD=Q2243}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949}} : 72 {{ill|lt=Sumadera|Q11664679|WD=Q11664679}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{ill|lt=Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|WD=Q11484399|lt=Hirohata Shrine}} === Other === Until {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{ill|lt=July|Q121|WD=Q121}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{ill|lt=Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349|WD=Q4700349}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and category|Category:Watatsumi-jinja (Kobe)}} [[EN:Watatsumi Shrine (Kobe)]] [[JA:海神社 (神戸市)]] [[SIMPLE:Watatsumi Shrine (Kobe)]] {{wikidata link|Q11559098}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Watatsumi Shrine |Image = [[File:Watatsumi-jinja (Tarumi, Kobe), haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}{{ill|lt=Kobe City|Q48320|WD=Q48320}}{{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806}} Miyamoto-chō 5-1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami
Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami
Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanpei Chūsha|UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274}} |Founded = (Traditionally) during the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} style, copper roofing |Other names = Hyūga Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage = {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949}} No. 73 (Hyōgo No. 8)
One of the Three Great Shrines of Harima |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 11|Q2927|WD=Q2927}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Watatsumi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Watatsumi Shrine''' (わたつみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Miyamoto-chō, {{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe City|Q48320|WD=Q48320}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}), and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanpei Chūsha|UNKNOWN}}. Today, it is listed as a {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274}} of the {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077|WD=Q908077}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Iwa Shrine|Q65320|WD=Q65320}} and {{ill|lt=Tsuburaiza Amateru Shrine|Q11604472|WD=Q11604472}}, it is considered one of the Three Great Shrines of {{ill|lt=Harima|Q3783364|WD=Q3783364}}. === Overview === In notifications under the Religious Corporations Act, the shrine is read as “Watatsumi Jinja,” but the reading “Kaijinja” is also widely known among local residents and parishioners{{Cite web |title=How do you read Kobe’s “Watatsumi Shrine”? “Kai,” “Ama,” “Watatsumi”… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|766190/1/21|Sanyō Railway Passenger Guide: also called Illustrated Guide to Famous Places along the Sanyō Road|format=EXTERNAL}}. In ancient times it was called Amajinja or Tarumijinja, as well as Hyūga Daimyōjin and Izaida Daimyōjin. The reading “Watatsumi” was adopted in Meiji 4 (1871) based on the theory of {{ill|lt=Motoori Norinaga|Q384647|WD=Q384647}}. The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|WD=Q1474187|lt=sea deities}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} was returning from the {{ill|lt=Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}} for {{ill|lt=Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086}}, {{ill|lt=Nagata Shrine|Q661395|WD=Q661395}}, and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinpu|UNKNOWN}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{ill|lt=Akashi Domain|Q839406|WD=Q839406}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokuhei Chūsha|UNKNOWN}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{ill|lt=Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297|WD=Q705297}}, {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{ill|lt=January 10|Q2243|WD=Q2243}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949}} : 72 {{ill|lt=Sumadera|Q11664679|WD=Q11664679}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{ill|lt=Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|WD=Q11484399|lt=Hirohata Shrine}} === Other === Until {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{ill|lt=July|Q121|WD=Q121}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{ill|lt=Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349|WD=Q4700349}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and One of the Three Great Shrines of Harima |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 11|Q2927|WD=Q2927}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Watatsumi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Watatsumi Shrine''' (わたつみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Miyamoto-chō, {{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe City|Q48320|WD=Q48320}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}), and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanpei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. Today, it is listed as a {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274}} of the {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077|WD=Q908077}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Iwa Shrine|Q65320|WD=Q65320}} and {{ill|lt=Tsuburaiza Amateru Shrine|Q11604472|WD=Q11604472}}, it is considered one of the Three Great Shrines of {{ill|lt=Harima|Q3783364|WD=Q3783364}}. === Overview === In notifications under the Religious Corporations Act, the shrine is read as “Watatsumi Jinja,” but the reading “Kaijinja” is also widely known among local residents and parishioners{{Cite web |title=How do you read Kobe’s “Watatsumi Shrine”? “Kai,” “Ama,” “Watatsumi”… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|766190/1/21|Sanyō Railway Passenger Guide: also called Illustrated Guide to Famous Places along the Sanyō Road|format=EXTERNAL}}. In ancient times it was called Amajinja or Tarumijinja, as well as Hyūga Daimyōjin and Izaida Daimyōjin. The reading “Watatsumi” was adopted in Meiji 4 (1871) based on the theory of {{ill|lt=Motoori Norinaga|Q384647|WD=Q384647}}. The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|WD=Q1474187|lt=sea deities}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} was returning from the {{ill|lt=Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}} for {{ill|lt=Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086}}, {{ill|lt=Nagata Shrine|Q661395|WD=Q661395}}, and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinpu|1=UNKNOWN}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{ill|lt=Akashi Domain|Q839406|WD=Q839406}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokuhei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{ill|lt=Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297|WD=Q705297}}, {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{ill|lt=January 10|Q2243|WD=Q2243}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949}} : 72 {{ill|lt=Sumadera|Q11664679|WD=Q11664679}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{ill|lt=Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|WD=Q11484399|lt=Hirohata Shrine}} === Other === Until {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{ill|lt=July|Q121|WD=Q121}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{ill|lt=Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349|WD=Q4700349}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|WD=Q1474187|lt=sea deities}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} was returning from the {{ill|lt=Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}} for {{ill|lt=Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086}}, {{ill|lt=Nagata Shrine|Q661395|WD=Q661395}}, and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinpu|1=UNKNOWN}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{ill|lt=Akashi Domain|Q839406|WD=Q839406}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokuhei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{ill|lt=Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297|WD=Q705297}}, {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{ill|lt=January 10|Q2243|WD=Q2243}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949}} : 72 {{ill|lt=Sumadera|Q11664679|WD=Q11664679}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{ill|lt=Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|WD=Q11484399|lt=Hirohata Shrine}} === Other === Until {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{ill|lt=July|Q121|WD=Q121}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{ill|lt=Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349|WD=Q4700349}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and
