One of the Three Great Shrines of Harima |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 11|Q2927|WD=Q2927|1=October 11}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Watatsumi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Watatsumi Shrine''' (わたつみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Miyamoto-chō, {{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806|1=Tarumi-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe City|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanpei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. Today, it is listed as a {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of the {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Iwa Shrine|Q65320|WD=Q65320|1=Iwa Shrine (Matsumoto)}} and {{ill|lt=Tsuburaiza Amateru Shrine|Q11604472|WD=Q11604472|1=Iibo Nimasu Amaterasu Shrine}}, it is considered one of the Three Great Shrines of {{ill|lt=Harima|Q3783364|WD=Q3783364|1=Harima}}. === Overview === In notifications under the Religious Corporations Act, the shrine is read as “Watatsumi Jinja,” but the reading “Kaijinja” is also widely known among local residents and parishioners{{Cite web |title=How do you read Kobe’s “Watatsumi Shrine”? “Kai,” “Ama,” “Watatsumi”… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|766190/1/21|Sanyō Railway Passenger Guide: also called Illustrated Guide to Famous Places along the Sanyō Road|format=EXTERNAL}}. In ancient times it was called Amajinja or Tarumijinja, as well as Hyūga Daimyōjin and Izaida Daimyōjin. The reading “Watatsumi” was adopted in Meiji 4 (1871) based on the theory of {{ill|lt=Motoori Norinaga|Q384647|WD=Q384647|1=Motoori Norinaga}}. The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655|1=Ujigami}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|WD=Q1474187|lt=sea deities|1=Watatsumi}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto|1=Amaterasu}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} was returning from the {{ill|lt=Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435|1=Three Han Campaign}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} for {{ill|lt=Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine|1=Hirota Shrine (Osaka City)}}, {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086|1=Ikuta Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Nagata Shrine|Q661395|WD=Q661395|1=Nagata Shrine}}, and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinpu|1=UNKNOWN}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{ill|lt=Akashi Domain|Q839406|WD=Q839406|1=Akashi Domain}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokuhei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{ill|lt=Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297|WD=Q705297|1=Nishinomiya Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{ill|lt=January 10|Q2243|WD=Q2243|1=January 10}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} : 72 {{ill|lt=Sumadera|Q11664679|WD=Q11664679|1=Suma Temple}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{ill|lt=Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|WD=Q11484399|lt=Hirohata Shrine|1=Hiromine Shrine}} === Other === Until {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{ill|lt=July|Q121|WD=Q121|1=July}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{ill|lt=Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349|WD=Q4700349|1=Akashi pedestrian bridge accident}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and supervised by Motohiko Anzu and Yoshihiko Umeda, *Shinto Dictionary*, {{ill|lt=Jinja Shinpō|Q11590705|WD=Q11590705|1=Jinja Shinpō}}, 1968, p. 65 * Edited by {{ill|lt=Eiji Shirai|Q11579387|WD=Q11579387|1=Eiji Shirai}} and Masanori Toki, *Shrine Dictionary*, {{ill|lt=Tōkyōdō Publishing|Q11524645|WD=Q11524645|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan}}, 1979, p. 359 === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806|1=Tarumi-ku, Kobe}} * {{ill|lt=Tarumi Station|Q774385|WD=Q774385|1=Tarumi Station (Hyōgo)}} * {{ill|lt=Marinepia Kobe|Q11341124|WD=Q11341124|1=Marinepia Kobe}} / {{ill|lt=Mitsui Outlet Park Marinepia Kobe|Q11354184|WD=Q11354184|1=Mitsui Outlet Park Marinepia Kobe}} * {{ill|lt=Prico Tarumi|Q28683734|WD=Q28683734|1=Prico Tarumi}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shimohata Watatsumi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (former original shrine) === External Links === {{Commonscat|Watatsumi-jinja (Kobe)}} * [http://kaijinjya.jp/ Watatsumi Shrine] (official site) * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301189.html Watatsumi Shrine] (Hyōgo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines) * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/440501.html Watatsumi Shrine, three seats] (Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection”) File:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja torii (1).jpg|Great torii gate File:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja altar.jpg|Watatsumi Shrine, Treasure of the Sea Deity {{Shinto horizontal}} {{Authority control}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Watatsumi Shrine {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} |Founded = (Traditionally) during the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style, copper roofing |Other names = Hyūga Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage = {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} No. 73 (Hyōgo No. 8)
One of the Three Great Shrines of Harima |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 11|Q2927|WD=Q2927|1=October 11}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Watatsumi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Watatsumi Shrine''' (わたつみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Miyamoto-chō, {{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806|1=Tarumi-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe City|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanpei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. Today, it is listed as a {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of the {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Iwa Shrine|Q65320|WD=Q65320|1=Iwa Shrine (Matsumoto)}} and {{ill|lt=Tsuburaiza Amateru Shrine|Q11604472|WD=Q11604472|1=Iibo Nimasu Amaterasu Shrine}}, it is considered one of the Three Great Shrines of {{ill|lt=Harima|Q3783364|WD=Q3783364|1=Harima}}. === Overview === In notifications under the Religious Corporations Act, the shrine is read as “Watatsumi Jinja,” but the reading “Kaijinja” is also widely known among local residents and parishioners{{Cite web |title=How do you read Kobe’s “Watatsumi Shrine”? “Kai,” “Ama,” “Watatsumi”… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|766190/1/21|Sanyō Railway Passenger Guide: also called Illustrated Guide to Famous Places along the Sanyō Road|format=EXTERNAL}}. In ancient times it was called Amajinja or Tarumijinja, as well as Hyūga Daimyōjin and Izaida Daimyōjin. The reading “Watatsumi” was adopted in Meiji 4 (1871) based on the theory of {{ill|lt=Motoori Norinaga|Q384647|WD=Q384647|1=Motoori Norinaga}}. The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655|1=Ujigami}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|WD=Q1474187|lt=sea deities|1=Watatsumi}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto|1=Amaterasu}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} was returning from the {{ill|lt=Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435|1=Three Han Campaign}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} for {{ill|lt=Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine|1=Hirota Shrine (Osaka City)}}, {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086|1=Ikuta Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Nagata Shrine|Q661395|WD=Q661395|1=Nagata Shrine}}, and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinpu|1=UNKNOWN}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{ill|lt=Akashi Domain|Q839406|WD=Q839406|1=Akashi Domain}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokuhei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{ill|lt=Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297|WD=Q705297|1=Nishinomiya Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{ill|lt=January 10|Q2243|WD=Q2243|1=January 10}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} : 72 {{ill|lt=Sumadera|Q11664679|WD=Q11664679|1=Suma Temple}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{ill|lt=Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|WD=Q11484399|lt=Hirohata Shrine|1=Hiromine Shrine}} === Other === Until {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{ill|lt=July|Q121|WD=Q121|1=July}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{ill|lt=Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349|WD=Q4700349|1=Akashi pedestrian bridge accident}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and supervised by Motohiko Anzu and Yoshihiko Umeda, *Shinto Dictionary*, {{ill|lt=Jinja Shinpō|Q11590705|WD=Q11590705|1=Jinja Shinpō}}, 1968, p. 65 * Edited by {{ill|lt=Eiji Shirai|Q11579387|WD=Q11579387|1=Eiji Shirai}} and Masanori Toki, *Shrine Dictionary*, {{ill|lt=Tōkyōdō Publishing|Q11524645|WD=Q11524645|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan}}, 1979, p. 359 === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806|1=Tarumi-ku, Kobe}} * {{ill|lt=Tarumi Station|Q774385|WD=Q774385|1=Tarumi Station (Hyōgo)}} * {{ill|lt=Marinepia Kobe|Q11341124|WD=Q11341124|1=Marinepia Kobe}} / {{ill|lt=Mitsui Outlet Park Marinepia Kobe|Q11354184|WD=Q11354184|1=Mitsui Outlet Park Marinepia Kobe}} * {{ill|lt=Prico Tarumi|Q28683734|WD=Q28683734|1=Prico Tarumi}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shimohata Watatsumi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (former original shrine) === External Links === {{Commonscat|Watatsumi-jinja (Kobe)}} * [http://kaijinjya.jp/ Watatsumi Shrine] (official site) * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301189.html Watatsumi Shrine] (Hyōgo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines) * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/440501.html Watatsumi Shrine, three seats] (Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection”) File:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja torii (1).jpg|Great torii gate File:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja altar.jpg|Watatsumi Shrine, Treasure of the Sea Deity {{Shinto horizontal}} {{Authority control}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Watatsumi Shrine Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami

Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanpei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} |Founded = (Traditionally) during the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style, copper roofing |Other names = Hyūga Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage = {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} No. 73 (Hyōgo No. 8)
One of the Three Great Shrines of Harima |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 11|Q2927|WD=Q2927|1=October 11}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Watatsumi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Watatsumi Shrine''' (わたつみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Miyamoto-chō, {{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806|1=Tarumi-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe City|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanpei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. Today, it is listed as a {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of the {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Iwa Shrine|Q65320|WD=Q65320|1=Iwa Shrine (Matsumoto)}} and {{ill|lt=Tsuburaiza Amateru Shrine|Q11604472|WD=Q11604472|1=Iibo Nimasu Amaterasu Shrine}}, it is considered one of the Three Great Shrines of {{ill|lt=Harima|Q3783364|WD=Q3783364|1=Harima}}. === Overview === In notifications under the Religious Corporations Act, the shrine is read as “Watatsumi Jinja,” but the reading “Kaijinja” is also widely known among local residents and parishioners{{Cite web |title=How do you read Kobe’s “Watatsumi Shrine”? “Kai,” “Ama,” “Watatsumi”… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|766190/1/21|Sanyō Railway Passenger Guide: also called Illustrated Guide to Famous Places along the Sanyō Road|format=EXTERNAL}}. In ancient times it was called Amajinja or Tarumijinja, as well as Hyūga Daimyōjin and Izaida Daimyōjin. The reading “Watatsumi” was adopted in Meiji 4 (1871) based on the theory of {{ill|lt=Motoori Norinaga|Q384647|WD=Q384647|1=Motoori Norinaga}}. The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655|1=Ujigami}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|WD=Q1474187|lt=sea deities|1=Watatsumi}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto|1=Amaterasu}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} was returning from the {{ill|lt=Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435|1=Three Han Campaign}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} for {{ill|lt=Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine|1=Hirota Shrine (Osaka City)}}, {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086|1=Ikuta Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Nagata Shrine|Q661395|WD=Q661395|1=Nagata Shrine}}, and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinpu|1=UNKNOWN}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{ill|lt=Akashi Domain|Q839406|WD=Q839406|1=Akashi Domain}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokuhei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{ill|lt=Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297|WD=Q705297|1=Nishinomiya Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{ill|lt=January 10|Q2243|WD=Q2243|1=January 10}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} : 72 {{ill|lt=Sumadera|Q11664679|WD=Q11664679|1=Suma Temple}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{ill|lt=Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|WD=Q11484399|lt=Hirohata Shrine|1=Hiromine Shrine}} === Other === Until {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{ill|lt=July|Q121|WD=Q121|1=July}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{ill|lt=Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349|WD=Q4700349|1=Akashi pedestrian bridge accident}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and supervised by Motohiko Anzu Shrine (Kobe)]] {{wikidata link|Q11559098}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Watatsumi Shrine |Image = [[File:Watatsumi-jinja (Tarumi, Kobe), haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kobe City|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}{{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806|1=Tarumi-ku, Kobe}} Miyamoto-chō 5-1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami
Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami
Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha|1=Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanpei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} |Founded = (Traditionally) during the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style, copper roofing |Other names = Hyūga Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage = {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} No. 73 (Hyōgo No. 8)
One of the Three Great Shrines of Harima |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 11|Q2927|WD=Q2927|1=October 11}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Watatsumi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Watatsumi Shrine''' (わたつみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Miyamoto-chō, {{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806|1=Tarumi-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe City|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanpei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. Today, it is listed as a {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of the {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Iwa Shrine|Q65320|WD=Q65320|1=Iwa Shrine (Matsumoto)}} and {{ill|lt=Tsuburaiza Amateru Shrine|Q11604472|WD=Q11604472|1=Iibo Nimasu Amaterasu Shrine}}, it is considered one of the Three Great Shrines of {{ill|lt=Harima|Q3783364|WD=Q3783364|1=Harima}}. === Overview === In notifications under the Religious Corporations Act, the shrine is read as “Watatsumi Jinja,” but the reading “Kaijinja” is also widely known among local residents and parishioners{{Cite web |title=How do you read Kobe’s “Watatsumi Shrine”? “Kai,” “Ama,” “Watatsumi”… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|766190/1/21|Sanyō Railway Passenger Guide: also called Illustrated Guide to Famous Places along the Sanyō Road|format=EXTERNAL}}. In ancient times it was called Amajinja or Tarumijinja, as well as Hyūga Daimyōjin and Izaida Daimyōjin. The reading “Watatsumi” was adopted in Meiji 4 (1871) based on the theory of {{ill|lt=Motoori Norinaga|Q384647|WD=Q384647|1=Motoori Norinaga}}. The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655|1=Ujigami}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|WD=Q1474187|lt=sea deities|1=Watatsumi}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto|1=Amaterasu}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} was returning from the {{ill|lt=Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435|1=Three Han Campaign}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} for {{ill|lt=Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine|1=Hirota Shrine (Osaka City)}}, {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086|1=Ikuta Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Nagata Shrine|Q661395|WD=Q661395|1=Nagata Shrine}}, and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinpu|1=UNKNOWN}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{ill|lt=Akashi Domain|Q839406|WD=Q839406|1=Akashi Domain}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokuhei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{ill|lt=Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297|WD=Q705297|1=Nishinomiya Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{ill|lt=January 10|Q2243|WD=Q2243|1=January 10}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} : 72 {{ill|lt=Sumadera|Q11664679|WD=Q11664679|1=Suma Temple}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{ill|lt=Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|WD=Q11484399|lt=Hirohata Shrine|1=Hiromine Shrine}} === Other === Until {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{ill|lt=July|Q121|WD=Q121|1=July}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{ill|lt=Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349|WD=Q4700349|1=Akashi pedestrian bridge accident}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and supervised by Motohiko Anzu and Yoshihiko Umeda, *Shinto Dictionary*, {{ill|lt=Jinja Shinpō|Q11590705|WD=Q11590705|1=Jinja Shinpō}}, 1968, p. 65 * Edited by {{ill|lt=Eiji Shirai|Q11579387|WD=Q11579387|1=Eiji Shirai}} and Masanori Toki, *Shrine Dictionary*, {{ill|lt=Tōkyōdō Publishing|Q11524645|WD=Q11524645|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan}}, 1979, p. 359 === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806|1=Tarumi-ku, Kobe}} * {{ill|lt=Tarumi Station|Q774385|WD=Q774385|1=Tarumi Station (Hyōgo)}} * {{ill|lt=Marinepia Kobe|Q11341124|WD=Q11341124|1=Marinepia Kobe}} / {{ill|lt=Mitsui Outlet Park Marinepia Kobe|Q11354184|WD=Q11354184|1=Mitsui Outlet Park Marinepia Kobe}} * {{ill|lt=Prico Tarumi|Q28683734|WD=Q28683734|1=Prico Tarumi}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shimohata Watatsumi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (former original shrine) === External Links === {{Commonscat|Watatsumi-jinja (Kobe)}} * [http://kaijinjya.jp/ Watatsumi Shrine] (official site) * Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kobe City|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}{{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806|1=Tarumi-ku, Kobe}} Miyamoto-chō 5-1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami
Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami
Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha|1=Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanpei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} |Founded = (Traditionally) during the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style, copper roofing |Other names = Hyūga Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage = {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} No. 73 (Hyōgo No. 8)
One of the Three Great Shrines of Harima |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 11|Q2927|WD=Q2927|1=October 11}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Watatsumi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Watatsumi Shrine''' (わたつみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Miyamoto-chō, {{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806|1=Tarumi-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe City|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanpei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. Today, it is listed as a {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of the {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Iwa Shrine|Q65320|WD=Q65320|1=Iwa Shrine (Matsumoto)}} and {{ill|lt=Tsuburaiza Amateru Shrine|Q11604472|WD=Q11604472|1=Iibo Nimasu Amaterasu Shrine}}, it is considered one of the Three Great Shrines of {{ill|lt=Harima|Q3783364|WD=Q3783364|1=Harima}}. === Overview === In notifications under the Religious Corporations Act, the shrine is read as “Watatsumi Jinja,” but the reading “Kaijinja” is also widely known among local residents and parishioners{{Cite web |title=How do you read Kobe’s “Watatsumi Shrine”? “Kai,” “Ama,” “Watatsumi”… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|766190/1/21|Sanyō Railway Passenger Guide: also called Illustrated Guide to Famous Places along the Sanyō Road|format=EXTERNAL}}. In ancient times it was called Amajinja or Tarumijinja, as well as Hyūga Daimyōjin and Izaida Daimyōjin. The reading “Watatsumi” was adopted in Meiji 4 (1871) based on the theory of {{ill|lt=Motoori Norinaga|Q384647|WD=Q384647|1=Motoori Norinaga}}. The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655|1=Ujigami}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|WD=Q1474187|lt=sea deities|1=Watatsumi}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto|1=Amaterasu}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} was returning from the {{ill|lt=Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435|1=Three Han Campaign}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} for {{ill|lt=Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine|1=Hirota Shrine (Osaka City)}}, {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086|1=Ikuta Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Nagata Shrine|Q661395|WD=Q661395|1=Nagata Shrine}}, and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinpu|1=UNKNOWN}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{ill|lt=Akashi Domain|Q839406|WD=Q839406|1=Akashi Domain}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokuhei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{ill|lt=Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297|WD=Q705297|1=Nishinomiya Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{ill|lt=January 10|Q2243|WD=Q2243|1=January 10}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} : 72 {{ill|lt=Sumadera|Q11664679|WD=Q11664679|1=Suma Temple}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{ill|lt=Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|WD=Q11484399|lt=Hirohata Shrine|1=Hiromine Shrine}} === Other === Until {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{ill|lt=July|Q121|WD=Q121|1=July}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{ill|lt=Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349|WD=Q4700349|1=Akashi pedestrian bridge accident}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and supervised by Motohiko Anzu and Yoshihiko Umeda, *Shinto Dictionary*, {{ill|lt=Jinja Shinpō|Q11590705|WD=Q11590705|1=Jinja Shinpō}}, 1968, p. 65 * Edited by {{ill|lt=Eiji Shirai|Q11579387|WD=Q11579387|1=Eiji Shirai}} and Masanori Toki, *Shrine Dictionary*, {{ill|lt=Tōkyōdō Publishing|Q11524645|WD=Q11524645|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan}}, 1979, p. 359 === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806|1=Tarumi-ku, Kobe}} * {{ill|lt=Tarumi Station|Q774385|WD=Q774385|1=Tarumi Station (Hyōgo)}} * {{ill|lt=Marinepia Kobe|Q11341124|WD=Q11341124|1=Marinepia Kobe}} / {{ill|lt=Mitsui Outlet Park Marinepia Kobe|Q11354184|WD=Q11354184|1=Mitsui Outlet Park Marinepia Kobe}} * {{ill|lt=Prico Tarumi|Q28683734|WD=Q28683734|1=Prico Tarumi}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shimohata Watatsumi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (former original shrine) === External Links === {{Commonscat|Watatsumi-jinja (Kobe)}} * [http://kaijinjya.jp/ Watatsumi Shrine] (official site) * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301189.html Watatsumi Shrine] (Hyōgo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines) * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/440501.html Watatsumi Shrine, three seats] (Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection”) File:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja torii (1).jpg|Great torii gate File:Kobe Watatsumi-jinja Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanpei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} |Founded = (Traditionally) during the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} |Main hall = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style, copper roofing |Other names = Hyūga Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage = {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} No. 73 (Hyōgo No. 8)
One of the Three Great Shrines of Harima |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 11|Q2927|WD=Q2927|1=October 11}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Watatsumi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Watatsumi Shrine''' (わたつみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Miyamoto-chō, {{ill|lt=Tarumi Ward|Q1078806|WD=Q1078806|1=Tarumi-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe City|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine rankings|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanpei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. Today, it is listed as a {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of the {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Iwa Shrine|Q65320|WD=Q65320|1=Iwa Shrine (Matsumoto)}} and {{ill|lt=Tsuburaiza Amateru Shrine|Q11604472|WD=Q11604472|1=Iibo Nimasu Amaterasu Shrine}}, it is considered one of the Three Great Shrines of {{ill|lt=Harima|Q3783364|WD=Q3783364|1=Harima}}. === Overview === In notifications under the Religious Corporations Act, the shrine is read as “Watatsumi Jinja,” but the reading “Kaijinja” is also widely known among local residents and parishioners{{Cite web |title=How do you read Kobe’s “Watatsumi Shrine”? “Kai,” “Ama,” “Watatsumi”… |url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/odekake-plus/news/detail.shtml?url=news/odekake-plus/news/pickup/201712/10819940 |website=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2017-12-14 |access-date=2025-07-03 |language=Japanese}}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|766190/1/21|Sanyō Railway Passenger Guide: also called Illustrated Guide to Famous Places along the Sanyō Road|format=EXTERNAL}}. In ancient times it was called Amajinja or Tarumijinja, as well as Hyūga Daimyōjin and Izaida Daimyōjin. The reading “Watatsumi” was adopted in Meiji 4 (1871) based on the theory of {{ill|lt=Motoori Norinaga|Q384647|WD=Q384647|1=Motoori Norinaga}}. The *History of Watatsumi Shrine, a Kanpei Chūsha of Harima Province* states that “according to ancient precedent, it should be read Ama or Tarumi.” The reading “Tarumi” derives from the fact that the original name of the enshrined deity was Tarumi-no-Kami. “Ama” comes from the shrine having been the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q767655|WD=Q767655|1=Ujigami}} (clan tutelary deity) of the Ama no Atai (sea-faring clan). === Enshrined Deities === As the three seats of {{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|WD=Q1474187|lt=sea deities|1=Watatsumi}}, the following three deities are enshrined as the principal deities, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Ōhirume no Muchi no Mikoto|1=Amaterasu}} enshrined as an associated deity. * Uwatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea surface = navigation * Nakatsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea depths = fish (fishing) * Sokotsuwatatsumi-no-Kami: deity of the sea bottom = seaweed and salt === History === According to shrine tradition, when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} was returning from the {{ill|lt=Three Han Expeditions|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435|1=Three Han Campaign}}, a violent storm arose at sea near this area and her ship was unable to proceed. When the Empress enshrined the three Watatsumi deities, the storm subsided, and in gratitude a shrine building was erected here to enshrine them. This account closely resembles the foundation legends recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} for {{ill|lt=Hirota Shrine|Q1466105|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine|1=Hirota Shrine (Osaka City)}}, {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086|1=Ikuta Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Nagata Shrine|Q661395|WD=Q661395|1=Nagata Shrine}}, and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, although the relevant passage in the *Nihon Shoki* does not mention Watatsumi Shrine. The earliest appearance in documents is in the *{{ill|lt=Shinshō Kyakufu-shō|Q11502312|WD=Q11502312|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō}}*, dated to Daidō 1 (806), which records the donation of ten {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinpu|1=UNKNOWN}} to Tarumi-no-Kami of Akashi in Harima Province. Because this site was a key point for maritime transportation, it was revered from early times as a deity protecting sea travel. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as “Watatsumi Shrine, three seats, Akashi District, Harima Province,” and ranked as a Myōjin Taisha. From the medieval period onward, the shrine declined due to warfare and other factors, but in Tenshō 11 (1587) {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} donated mountain forests in Tarumi-gō as prayer offerings. During the Edo period, successive lords of the {{ill|lt=Akashi Domain|Q839406|WD=Q839406|1=Akashi Domain}} revered the shrine deeply, making annual visits each February a customary practice. From the early Edo period it was called “Hyūga Daimyōjin”{{Cite book|Japanese|doi=10.11501/2563455|title=Banshū Meisho Junran Zue, Vol. 2|date=1804|chapterurl={{NDLDC|2563455/24}}|chapter=Tarumi Shrine, Hyūga Myōjin}}, but when it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokuhei Chūsha|1=UNKNOWN}} in Meiji 4 (1871), it reverted to the name “Watatsumi Shrine.” In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha. Until before the period of high economic growth, the area around the great torii gate was a sandy beach, making it literally a “shrine of the sea”. However, from 1960 onward land reclamation was carried out, and the area in front of the great torii became roads and a fishing port{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/kobe/202201/0014966735.shtml |title=Tarumi once had sandy beaches, Maiko had only a few pine trees—why did the scenery change? |publisher=Kobe Shimbun NEXT |date=2022-01-07 |accessdate=2022-01-07}}. === Rituals === === Ebisu Festival === As at {{ill|lt=Nishinomiya Shrine|Q705297|WD=Q705297|1=Nishinomiya Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} is also a sea deity, so the festival is held for three days around {{ill|lt=January 10|Q2243|WD=Q2243|1=January 10}} each year. === Preceding and Following Pilgrimage Sites === ; {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} : 72 {{ill|lt=Sumadera|Q11664679|WD=Q11664679|1=Suma Temple}} – '''73 Watatsumi Shrine''' – 74 {{ill|lt=Hirohata Shrine|Q11484399|WD=Q11484399|lt=Hirohata Shrine|1=Hiromine Shrine}} === Other === Until {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 18 (2006), a fireworks display dedicated to the shrine was held every {{ill|lt=July|Q121|WD=Q121|1=July}} during the summer festival, but from the following year onward, while the summer festival continued as before, the fireworks display was discontinued. {{Citation needed|date=January 2012|The {{ill|lt=Akashi Fireworks Festival Pedestrian Bridge Accident|Q4700349|WD=Q4700349|1=Akashi pedestrian bridge accident}} that occurred in Heisei 13 (2001) is believed to have had no small influence.}} === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === Related Books === * Edited and supervised by Motohiko Anzu and Yoshihiko Umeda, *Shinto Dictionary*, {{ill|lt=Jinja Shinpō|Q11590705|WD=Q11590705|1=Jinja Shinpō}}, 1968, p. 65 * Edited by {{ill|lt=Eiji Shirai|Q11579387|WD=Q11579387|1=Eiji Shirai}} and
